Styles › Scandinavian

1950s – present · Nordic Europe

Scandinavian

Simplicity, functionality, nature.

  • Overview
  • Design Philosophy
  • Visual Characteristics
  • Materials
  • Color Palette
  • Façade Guide
  • Lighting
  • Building Types

  • AI Prompts

  • Related

01 — Overview

Overview

Definition. Scandinavian architecture pairs clean, functional forms with natural materials and a deep connection to landscape and light — restrained exteriors of timber, glass and pale render under pitched or simple roofs.

Historical background. Rooted in Nordic functionalism and craft traditions, it balances Modernist clarity with warmth, daylight and the realities of a northern climate.

Origin. Denmark, Sweden, Norway, Finland.

Timeline

1950

Nordic functionalism matures with Aalto & Jacobsen

1970

Timber-and-glass houses spread

2008

Snøhetta's Oslo Opera House

2015

Global popularity of the Scandinavian house

Key Architects

Alvar AaltoArne JacobsenSnøhettaJensen & Skodvin

Influential Projects

Oslo Opera House

Snøhetta · 2008
Oslo, Norway

Finlandia Hall

Alvar Aalto · 1971
Helsinki, Finland

Juvet Landscape Hotel

Juvet Landscape Hotel

Jensen & Skodvin · 2008
Norway

02 — Design Philosophy

Design Philosophy

Core Principles

Functional simplicityNatural materialsDaylightConnection to natureUnderstated craft

The Thinking

Form serves everyday life with quiet care; exteriors are calm and warm, designed around scarce northern light and the surrounding landscape.

Values It Represents

ComfortHonestySustainabilityDemocratic design

Why It Emerged

Shaped by climate, long winters and a craft culture valuing modest, useful, beautiful things.

03 — Visual Characteristics

Visual Characteristics

MassingCompact, human-scaled volumes, sometimes gabled, settled into the land.
Form LanguageClean forms with pitched or simple flat roofs; gentle, unfussy.
CompositionCalm asymmetry; large openings toward light and view.
ProportionModest, comfortable, human.
GeometryOrthogonal with the archetypal gable silhouette.
RhythmQuiet timber cladding rhythm and regular windows.
SymmetryMostly asymmetrical, balanced.
ScaleDomestic and intimate.
Roof TypesPitched gables or simple flat roofs; clean eaves.
Window ProportionLarge windows to capture daylight; generous glazing to views.
EntranceSimple, sheltered, understated.
Façade ArticulationTimber cladding (often painted or charred), pale render and large glazing under clean roofs.

04 — Materials

Materials

Timber cladding

Warm, renewable, local — the signature Nordic skin.

Glass

Captures scarce daylight and frames landscape.

Light concrete

Quiet, durable base material.

Charred/painted wood

Black or deep-toned timber for contrast and protection.

05 — Color Palette

Color Palette

Light, warm, natural — soft neutrals grounded by dark timber.

Primary

Pale Timber
#d6bd92
Soft White
#efece4

Accent

Charcoal Wood
#3a3631
Forest Green
#46584b

Material Colors

Glass
#aec4c8
Light Concrete
#cfc9bd

Typical Combinations

  • +Pale timber + white + glass
  • +Charred black timber + warm wood

06 — Façade Design Guide

Façade Design Guide

Window CompositionLarge, generous openings oriented to light and view; clean frames.
EmphasisHorizontal timber cladding lines; calm and quiet.
Shading DevicesDeep eaves and timber screens; modest solar control.
BalconiesSimple timber decks and terraces blending indoors and out.
FramesSlim timber or dark metal frames.
OpeningsGenerous but composed; framing the landscape.
Architectural DetailsCrisp timber joinery, clean eaves, honest fixings.
OrnamentationNone — the grain and rhythm of timber is the decoration.

07 — Lighting

Lighting

Natural Lighting Strategy

Designed around low northern sun — big south glazing, light shelves, bright surfaces.

Artificial Lighting Concept

Warm, layered, low-glare lighting echoing cosy interiors onto terraces.

Night Façade Appearance

Warm timber-framed glow; gentle and inviting.

Recommendations

Warm 2700K; light timber to keep warmth; emphasise sheltered entrances.

08 — Typical Building Types

Typical Building Types

Where this style performs best:

Houses & cabinsCultural buildingsSchoolsHotels & retreatsOfficesLibraries

09 — Design Checklist

Design Checklist

Tap an item to mark it complete — saved to your browser.

  • Use natural timber cladding
  • Maximise daylight with large windows
  • Keep clean pitched or simple roofs
  • Settle the form into the landscape
  • Detail crisp timber joinery and eaves
  • Use a soft neutral palette with dark-wood accent
  • Add sheltered decks/terraces
  • Keep everything understated and functional

10 — Common Mistakes

Common Mistakes

Elements that contradict the style — what to avoid:

  • ✕Fussy ornament or heavy detailing
  • ✕Small windows that ignore daylight needs
  • ✕Synthetic finishes replacing natural timber
  • ✕Ostentatious scale breaking the domestic calm
  • ✕Cold palettes with no warmth

11 — AI Prompt Examples

AI Prompt Examples

Optimised for Midjourney, ChatGPT image tools or Claude — exterior renders. Tap copy to use.

Exterior

Scandinavian house exterior, warm timber cladding, large windows, clean gabled roof, set in a forest landscape, soft northern daylight, architectural photography --ar 3:2 --style raw

Façade Concept

Scandinavian façade, horizontal timber cladding, generous glazing, slim dark frames, charred-wood accent, clean eaves, frontal elevation, soft light

Night View

Scandinavian house exterior at night, warm glow through large windows, timber façade, snow, cosy and inviting, architectural night photography

Aerial View

Aerial of a Scandinavian retreat, timber-clad volumes amid forest and water, pitched roofs, natural palette, drone photography

Street Perspective

Street view of a Scandinavian home exterior, timber cladding, sheltered entrance, large windows, calm, documentary photography, natural light

Masterplan

Scandinavian masterplan, timber houses settled along terrain and water, generous green space, pitched roofs, axonometric, natural palette
